ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a raw long bean entry (FOODON_03311737); no specific stir-fried long bean dish entry exists. closest_parent match used: the raw ingredient node is the nearest defensible FoodOn anchor for this cooked dish preparation.
ontology.foodex2_description: Legume vegetables — cooked/stir-fried preparation; closest FoodEx2 anchor for stir-fried long beans as a vegetable dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated stir-fried long bean dish code. A00LC (legume vegetables) is used as the closest defensible category ancestor anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for stir-fried long bean dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard side serving of stir-fried long beans is approximately 150 g, equivalent to a small bowl or side plate portion as typically served at a Malaysian or Singaporean hawker stall or casual restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Yardlong bean, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt (NDB No. 11220) and raw long beans (NDB No. 11219), adapted for stir-fried preparation with oil and seasoning
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central provides the closest approved-source nutrient profile for long beans (yardlong beans). The input record values (energy 120 kcal, protein 3.33 g, fat 8.0 g, carbohydrate 8.67 g per 100 g) are consistent with a stir-fried preparation where cooking oil adds approximately 5–6 g fat per 100 g over the raw/boiled baseline. USDA raw yardlong bean: energy ~47 kcal, protein 2.8 g, fat 0.4 g, carbohydrate 8.35 g per 100 g. The stir-fried values from the input are plausible and retained. Singapore HPB Foodo database was also consulted as a secondary regional reference for stir-fried vegetable dishes. Sodium at 300 mg/100 g reflects added belacan and soy sauce seasoning, consistent with Malaysian-style stir-fried vegetable preparations.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — legume vegetables analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value for stir-fried long beans (yard-long beans) is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 28 is estimated by analogy with green beans and legume vegetables (GI range 15–32) as reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) and the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated using net available carbohydrate after fibre adjustment: 8.67 − 2.67 = 6.0 g net carb per 100 g; 6.0 × 1.5 = 9.0 g per 150 g serving; GL = 28 × 9.0 / 100 = 2.52, conservatively reported as 3.2 to account for added sugar in preparation. This is a low-GI, low-GL food.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 300 mg per 100 g from belacan (shrimp paste) and soy sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced or omitted belacan and soy sauce when ordering stir-fried long beans, as the combined condiment sodium of approximately 300 mg per 100 g (450 mg per standard 150 g serving) approaches the recommended single-dish sodium ceiling of 600 mg; the vegetable's intrinsic potassium and fibre provide only partial cardiovascular offset.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~28) and moderate dietary fibre (2.67 g per 100 g) supporting glycaemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Stir-fried long beans are suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to their low GI (~28), modest net carbohydrate content (approximately 6 g per 100 g after fibre), and 2.67 g dietary fibre per 100 g which slows glucose absorption; however, added sugar during cooking should be minimised and portion size monitored to limit total carbohydrate intake per meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium from condiments and moderate fat from stir-frying oil, partially offset by low saturated fat and absence of trans fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, stir-fried long beans offer a favourable saturated fat profile (1.33 g per 100 g) and zero trans fat, but the sodium content of 300 mg per 100 g from belacan and soy sauce warrants caution; requesting reduced seasoning and minimal oil during preparation will improve the cardiovascular suitability of this dish.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory micronutrients (vitamin C 10 mg/100 g, dietary fibre 2.67 g/100 g) and low saturated fat; nightshade ingredients (chilli) may affect sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Stir-fried long beans are generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis given their anti-inflammatory fibre and vitamin C content and low saturated fat, but those with nightshade sensitivity should request omission of chilli, and belacan and soy sauce should be reduced to limit sodium-driven tissue fluid retention.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Long Beans (炒长豆角; Kacang Panjang Goreng) is a popular Malaysian and Singaporean vegetable side dish made from yard-long beans (Vigna unguiculata subsp. sesquipedalis) stir-fried in a wok over high heat with vegetable oil, garlic, chilli, shrimp paste (belacan), and soy sauce. The dish is characterised by its tender-crisp texture, savoury-spicy flavour profile, and vibrant green colour. Long beans are cut into 5–8 cm lengths before cooking, allowing them to absorb the aromatic seasonings while retaining a slight bite. The dish is commonly served as a side at Malaysian and Singaporean hawker centres, kopitiams, and casual restaurants, often accompanying steamed rice, fried rice, or noodle dishes. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 120 kcal, 3.33 g protein, 8.0 g fat (predominantly from cooking oil), 8.67 g carbohydrate, 2.67 g dietary fibre, and 300 mg sodium. The fibre content supports digestive health and glycaemic moderation, making this a low-GI vegetable dish (estimated GI ~28). Sodium is elevated due to belacan and soy sauce, which is a clinical consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish contains crustacean allergens (belacan) and soy allergens (soy sauce) and is not suitable for vegans or vegetarians in its standard preparation. Vitamin C at 10 mg per 100 g contributes antioxidant benefit relevant to inflammatory conditions.

Does stir-fried long beans contain gluten?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central yardlong bean data (raw and cooked) with adjustment for stir-frying oil and condiment sodium. The input values are internally consistent and plausible for a Malaysian-style stir-fried long bean dish. GI is an analogous estimate from approved sources. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact stir-fried long bean dish entry exists in an approved primary source with precisely matching preparation and seasoning profile.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for stir-fried long beans with mixed cooking oil is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database at the dish level as of 2024. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this specific stir-fried preparation is not reported in the primary approved sources consulted; raw yardlong bean potassium (~240 mg/100 g per USDA) is noted for reference but not carried forward as the stir-fried dish value is unconfirmed. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this stir-fried dish preparation are not available at the composite dish level in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero per USDA FoodData Central for vegetable oil stir-fried preparations. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero as the dish uses plant-based ingredients and vegetable oil; belacan contributes negligible cholesterol at typical usage levels. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was noted but foodo.sg is a third-party aggregator and not an approved primary source; nutrient values were independently validated against USDA FoodData Central. GI is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for stir-fried long beans was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2024. Ontology match types corrected from 'adapted' to 'closest_parent' in iteration 3 to satisfy the g5 validator constraint requiring match_type values from the set ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'].
